name: Mamuthones
dates: January 17 (Sant'Antonio Abate), Carnival Sunday, and Shrove Tuesday (February/March)
description: Pre-Christian masquerades dating back more than 2,000 years, believed to be one of the oldest Carnival traditions in the world. Twelve Mamuthones (symbolizing the twelve months) dress in black sheepskin with dark brown wooden masks and carry ~30 kg of cowbells tied to their backs with leather laces. They parade in two parallel lines through Mamoiada's narrow streets, producing a haunting rhythmic clang. The ritual is a propitiatory ceremony to intercede with natural forces at the seasonal crossing from dark winter to spring. 'The uglier [the mask], the better,' says the curator of the Museum of the Mediterranean Masks.

name: Issohadores
dates: January 17 (Sant'Antonio Abate), Carnival Sunday, and Shrove Tuesday (February/March)
description: Lithesome companions to the Mamuthones in the Mamoiada carnival. Issohadores (named after the 'soha' / lasso) wear bright red and white costumes reminiscent of Spanish colonial influence, and blank white unsmiling masks. There are usually 8–10 Issohadores, who guard the Mamuthones during the parade and seize 'preys' (mainly young women) with their lassos.

name: Sartiglia
description: Spectacular equestrian tournament held in Oristano during Carnival. Knights in elaborate costumes attempt to spear a star-shaped target while galloping at full speed. The event combines medieval Iberian tournament traditions with Sardinian folk ceremony.

name: Boes and Merdules
description: Ancient Carnival masks from Ottana, representing a dialogue between man (Merdules) and beast (Boes / bulls). Participants wearing bull masks (Boes) are led on ropes by human-masked figures (Merdules). The ritual symbolizes the taming of nature and is believed to have Nuragic-era origins.

name: Thurpos (Blind Men)
description: Carnival tradition in Orotelli featuring the 'Thurpos' (blind men) masked figures — costumed participants representing figures unable to see. Part of the wider family of ancient Nuragic-rooted Sardinian carnival masks.
